name: Early Repolarisation
identifyBy: J-point elevation (slur or notch at end of QRS) - typically 1–3mm in precordial leads V2–V5
Concave-up (saddle-back) ST elevation following the J-point elevation
'Fish-hook' appearance in lateral leads - notch on descending limb of R wave
Most common in young, athletic, Afro-Caribbean males
ST elevation is stable over time - does NOT evolve like STEMI

measurements.stSegment: 1–2mm J-point elevation, concave-up - V2–V5 most prominent

management: No treatment required in most cases - benign finding in majority
Documentation and reassurance: 'This is a common normal variant in young healthy adults'
High-risk ER: inferior leads (II, III, aVF) + horizontal/downsloping ST after J-point - slightly increased VF risk (rare)
Avoid confusion with STEMI: key features distinguish (no evolution, no symptoms, no reciprocal changes)
In context of chest pain: rule out STEMI before attributing to ER
Cardiology referral if: syncope, family history of SCD, high-risk ER pattern
pearls: ER is present in up to 5–10% of young adults - overwhelmingly benign
The 'fish-hook' notch on R wave descending limb is characteristic and reassuring
High-risk ER: horizontal or downsloping ST segment in inferior leads + notch - small increased SCD risk
Compared to pericarditis: ER has no PR depression, no pain evolution, and affects younger patients
STEMI vs ER: STEMI evolves over hours with reciprocal changes; ER is static over years
differentials: STEMI (evolving ST, convex morphology, reciprocal changes, chest pain)
Pericarditis (PR depression, diffuse involvement, pleuritic pain)
Brugada pattern (coved morphology in V1–V2 only, not precordial)
Normal variant ECG (difficult to distinguish - clinical context essential)
